coredata.dc:title: Perturbation effect of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots (rGOQDs) on aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathway in zebrafish
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jing-Hui
Sun, Tai
Niu, Aping
Tang, Yu-Mei
Deng, Shun
Luo, Wei
Xu, Qun
Wei, Dapeng
Pei, De-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been widely used in enormous fields, however, the inherent molecular mechanism of GQDs for potential risks in biological system is still elusive to date. In this study, the outstanding reduced graphene quantum dots (rGOQDs) with the QY as high as 24.62% were successfully synthesized by the improved Hummers method and DMF hydrothermal treatment approach. The rGOQDs were N-doped photoluminescent nanomaterials with functional groups on the surface. The fluorescent bio-imaging was performed by exposing zebrafish in different concentrations of the as-prepared rGOQDs, and the distribution of rGOQDs was successfully observed. Moreover, the developmental toxicity and genotoxicity were evaluated to further investigate the potential hazard of rGOQDs. The result indicated that rGOQDs were responsible for the dose-dependent abnormalities on the development of zebrafish. Since the real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) results showed that the expression of cyp1a was the highest expression in the selected genes and significantly up-regulated 8.49 fold in zebrafish, the perturbation of rGOQDs on aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathway was investigated by using the Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish for the first time. The results demonstrated that rGOQDs significantly increased the green fluorescent protein (GFP) expression promoted by cyp1a in a dose-dependent manner, which was also further confirmed by the western blotting. This study offered an opportunity to reveal the potential hazards of in vivo bio-probes, which provided a valuable reference for investigating the graphene-based materials on the disturbance of AhR pathway in biological organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AhR pathway
Bio-imaging
Reduced graphene oxide quantum dots
Real-time PCR
Zebrafish

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials at the Nanoscale for Diagnostic Systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exposure and fluorescence record of rGOQDs in zebrafish
Total RNAs isolation and quantitative RT-PCR analysis
FT-IR and XPS characterization of rGOQDs
UV–vis and PL analysis of rGOQDs
Distribution and bio-imaging of rGOQDs in zebrafish
Developmental toxicity and the perturbation of AhR pathway induced by rGOQDs in zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis route and bio-imaging of rGOQDs.

The chemical characterization of graphite, GO and rGOQDs. (A) FT-IR spectra, (B) XPS spectra and (C) N1s profile of rGOQDs. (D) C1s profile of graphite, (E) C1s profile of GO, (F) C1s profile of rGOQDs, (G) O1s profile of graphite, (H) O1s profile of GO, and (I) O1s profile of rGOQDs.

The PL characterization of rGOQDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra; (B) the normalized emission spectra of rGOQDs; (C) fluorescent luminescence emission spectra of GO; (D) fluorescent luminescence emission spectra of rGOQDs, with progressively longer excitation wavelengths in 20 nm increments from 300 nm to 500 nm. Inset: the fluorescent images of GO and rGOQDs in deionized water solution under the UV light.

The longtime fluorescent imaging of zebrafish from 12 hpf to 144 hpf after exposure to 100 μg/mL rGOQDs solution and control. (A) Bright field; (B–D) fluorescent field (B-blue, C-green, and D-red). (E–H) Control group (0 μg/mL) of zebrafish fluorescent imaging. Scale bars, 1000 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The developmental toxicity and genotoxicity induced by rGOQDs in zebrafish. (A) normal larvae; (B–D) abnormal larvae. Malformations were indicated by red arrows. PE, pericardial edema; VC, vitelline cyst; BS, bent spine; (E) hatch rates; (F) heart beats; (G) body length (H) mortality of zebrafish (n = 50). And the RNA expressions levels of cyp1a (I), cyp1c (J), cyp7a1 (K) and hsp70 (L) of zebrafish larvae were detected by RT-PCR after exposure to 0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL rGOQDs. The result were means ± standard error (SEM) of three replicate samples. Stars indicate the level of significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). The diagrams were produced using GraphPad Prism (Version 5.0). Scale bar = 1000 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Analysis of GFP expression in Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish. (A) The fluorescent imaging of Tg(cyp1a:gfp) and AB strain zebrafish at 24, 48 and 120 hpf after exposure to rGOQDs solution. (B) The relative fluorescence intensity of AB and Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish after treated with different concentrations (n = 10). (C) The western blot analysis of GFP expression induced by rGOQDs in Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish. (D) Schematic representation of the perturbation effect of rGOQDs on the AhR pathway in zebrafish. The result were means ± standard error (SEM) of three replicate samples. Stars indicated the level of significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001). Scale bar = 500 μm.
Element contents and percentage of chemical bond of the graphene, GO and rGOQDs.
Primers for quantitative real-time PCR used in this study.
The developmental toxicity studies of zebrafish after exposure to different concentrations of rGOQDs.

head.ce:title: Perturbation effect of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots (rGOQDs) on aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathway in zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chongqing 400714, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been widely used in enormous fields, however, the inherent molecular mechanism of GQDs for potential risks in biological system is still elusive to date. In this study, the outstanding reduced graphene quantum dots (rGOQDs) with the QY as high as 24.62% were successfully synthesized by the improved Hummers method and DMF hydrothermal treatment approach. The rGOQDs were N-doped photoluminescent nanomaterials with functional groups on the surface. The fluorescent bio-imaging was performed by exposing zebrafish in different concentrations of the as-prepared rGOQDs, and the distribution of rGOQDs was successfully observed. Moreover, the developmental toxicity and genotoxicity were evaluated to further investigate the potential hazard of rGOQDs. The result indicated that rGOQDs were responsible for the dose-dependent abnormalities on the development of zebrafish. Since the real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) results showed that the expression of cyp1a was the highest expression in the selected genes and significantly up-regulated 8.49 fold in zebrafish, the perturbation of rGOQDs on aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathway was investigated by using the Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish for the first time. The results demonstrated that rGOQDs significantly increased the green fluorescent protein (GFP) expression promoted by cyp1a in a dose-dependent manner, which was also further confirmed by the western blotting. This study offered an opportunity to reveal the potential hazards of in vivo bio-probes, which provided a valuable reference for investigating the graphene-based materials on the disturbance of AhR pathway in biological organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a type of promising graphene-based photoluminescent nanomaterials, have gained tremendous research attention because of their unique and novel optical properties as well as exceptional physicochemical properties [1,2]. As the special structure of two-dimensional layers of sp2 hybridization carbon atoms, the graphene-based materials possess intriguing properties including extraordinary electrical conductivities, favorable mechanical properties and superior chemical stability [3]. Therefore, the novel graphene quantum dots derived from graphene not only inherited the structure and properties of graphene but also additionally possessed tunable photoluminescence (PL) nature, chemical inertness and particular electronic properties. Currently, GQDs have been regarded as the promising alternative fluorophores for bioimaging and optical sensing. Up to now, they have been largely applied in many application fields, such as biomedical optical imaging [4], heterojunction solar cells [5], supercapacitors [6,7], biosensing [8], and environmental monitoring [9]. As the glowing graphene nanoparticles has provided unprecedented opportunities for large application in optical-related fields, a variety of endeavors were carried out to prepare for GQDs. Previous literature have reported that the GQDs have been fabricated by the top-down approach of fragmenting the graphene sheets to small graphitic particles, and the bottom-up approach of synthesizing large graphene-like particles from organic chemists [9]. Moreover, as the graphite powder is hydrophobic and no luminescence in nature, the functionalization processes such as oxidation and doping were performed to turn the optical property and water solubility [10]. Herein, large quantities of endeavors had been made to obtain graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) through a oxidation route of graphite [11,12], and a series of approaches of functionalization including nitrogen-doped [13], photochemical reduction pathway [14] and fluorinated processes [15] were carried out to enhance the photoluminescence property of GQDs. On the basis of the previous studies, the method of top-down approach and subsequently modification processes were performed in this study, and the strong photoluminescent reduced graphene quantum dots (rGOQDs) was synthesized through the facile and effective oxidation, reduction and nitrogen-doped approaches.

Although the GQDs hold so many intriguing optical and physical properties, and be extensively studied in plenty of fields, the ecosystem risks and health interferences induced by GQDs were elusive. Before applying GQDs in bioluminescence imaging, their safety in bio-applications should be confirmed. There have been many models used to evaluate the biological compatibility and toxicity of GQDs both in vitro and in vivo, such as mouse [16], cells [17,18], bacteria [19], and zebrafish [20]. Among them, zebrafish (Danio Rerio) is one of the most powerful and promising in vivo model for studying on toxicity and genetics, due to it holds large numbers of advantages, such as low husbandry cost, short reproductive cycle, transparent embryos and facile genetic expression [21,22]. Up to now, zebrafish has been used as a popular model to study wastewater toxicity [23], DNA damage and repair [24], toxicity testing of nutraceuticals [25], drug screening and validation [26], PL imaging and biocompatibility study of carbon quantum dots (CDs) [27,28] and GQDs [20,29]. Hence, the zebrafish model is appropriate for assessing the toxicity of rGOQDs in this study.

The aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) pathway is responsible for regulating the activity of xenobiotics [30], and it mediates the regulation of cytochrome P450 enzymes as well as leads the transcriptional activation of CYP1A, which plays a key role in physiological immunity [31]. Due to graphene possess the aromatic hydrocarbon skeleton [32], it is important to study the effect of graphene-based materials on AhR pathway of living organisms. Although the previous literature had investigated the developmental toxicity [20] and cardiovascular toxicity [29] of GQDs, the toxicity evaluation of rGOQDs on transcriptional and translational level had not been studied yet. Thus, the gene and protein expression interferences of rGOQDs on zebrafish were the key research highlight in this study.

Here, the facile and efficient improved Hummer's method and the (N, N-dimethylformamide) DMF hydrothermal treatment approach were used to synthesize the strong green photoluminance rGOQDs. The micromorphology and chemical properties of the rGOQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR). The quantum yield (QY) of the rGOQDs was as high as 24.62%, which was calculated by using quinine sulfate as the standard. The strong luminous rGOQDs enabled the visualization of observing the spatial–temporal progression of fluorescence distribution in zebrafish by incubating zebrafish embryos in different concentrations of rGOQDs. Subsequently, the perturbation effect of rGOQDs on AhR pathway were tested by using wild-type zebrafish and Tg(cyp1a:gfp) transgenic zebrafish. The developmental toxicity was studied through the general indicators of hatch rate, heart beats, malformation and mortality. The transcriptional expressions of cyp1a, cyp1c, cyp7a1 and hsp70 were investigated by real-time PCR in wild-type zebrafish. In addition, the translational expression of cyp1a induced by rGOQDs was detected by green fluorescent protein (GFP) expression and the western blot (WB).

All zebrafish experiments were performed according to the “Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals” (Eighth Edition, 2011. ILARCLS, National Research Council, Washington, D.C.). The animal protocol was approved by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Approval ID: ZKCQY00120).

Graphite powder (about 10 nm in size) was purchased from XF NANO (China), and the concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). Sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Deionized water (18 MΩ/cm) for solution preparation was produced using the thermo scientific nanopure water purifier (Thermo, USA). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade.

The GO precursor were synthesized from graphite powder (10 nm, XF NANO) according to the modified Hummer's method [33]. Firstly, 60 mL concentrated H2SO4 (98%) were placed in 250 mL three-necked round-bottomed flask with gently stirring in an ice-water bath, and then added the mixture of 1 g graphene powder and 0.5 g sodium nitrate. After that, 6 g potassium permanganate was slowly added under vigorous stirring for 2 h in the ice-water bath with temperature at 10–15 °C. Subsequently, the ice-water bath was removed and the reaction system was turned into the oil-bath under continuous stirring for another 2 h with the temperature at 32–38 °C. Later, 48 mL deionized water was added dropwise and followed by raising the temperature to 98 °C with continuous stirring for 30 min. Then, 60 mL deionized water was added. After cooled down the temperature, 10 mL H2O2 (30%) was added to the reaction to obtain a luminous yellow solution. The impurity ions were removed by pumping filtration after adding 200 mL HCl (5%). Finally, the filtered solid was dissolved in the deionized water, centrifuged and washed to neutral. The suspension was dried by lyophilizing (Lyophilizer, Virtis BT4KXL, USA) and the gray-yellow powder GO was obtained.

The rGOQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of GO with DMF. About 0.5 g GO was suspended in 20 mL DMF solution and then treated by ultrasonic cell crusher (Scientz-IIF, China) for 5 min to disperse the suspension. The suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooled the reaction to room temperature, the large size black precipitates were removed by using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, the light yellow supernatant was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove the impurities. Finally, the rotary evaporation was used to evaporate the solvent and the strong green fluorescence rGOQDs were achieved.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties of rGOQDs was investigated by the FL-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The size distribution and morphologies of rGOQDs nanoparticles were examined by the field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra was recorded by the UV spectrophotometer (Lambda35, UK). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the products were recorded on Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer (USA), and the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a K-Alpha electron energy spectrometer (PHI-5400, USA) with a monochromated Al Kα X-ray source (hʋ = 1486.6 eV). After obtained the data from spectrometer, it was analyzed by XPS peak software, the peaks were resolved into different chemical bonds. Then, the integral area of each peak was calculated to obtain the chemical bond mapping, which was shown in the Table 1
. The brightfield and fluorescent images were acquired by the Nikon SMZ18 NIS-Elements BR Stereo Fluorescence Microscope (Nikon, Japan).

The QY detection test of the rGOQDs were performed by using the quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QY = 0.54) as standard, and the QY was calculated according by the following equation [34].
Q
Y x
= Q
Y
s t
(
K x
/
K
s t
)
(
η x
/
η
s t
)
2
where QY is the quantum yield; K is the slope of linear fitted by a series of PL intensity data (λex = 340 nm, λem = 380 nm) obtained at the corresponding absorbance values (less than 0.1 at 340 nm). The results were shown in Fig. S1, and η is the refractive index (1.33 for DI water and 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution). The subscript st refers to the quinine sulfate standard, and x refers to the samples. For these aqueous solutions, the
η x
/
η
s t
= 1
. A series of concentrations with absorbance values less than 0.1 for the references, and the samples were measured to obtain the slopes. The calculated results of the QYs were shown in Table S1.

Wild-type strains (AB) and Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish were raised according to standard husbandry protocols at 28 °C, with a 14 h light/10 h dark cycle. The Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish were modified from original ones that kindly gifted by Prof. Seok-Yong Choi [35]. The zebrafish were fed twice daily with decapsulated freshly hatched brine shrimps (Brine Shrimp Direct, USA). Before the start of a test, three male and two female adult zebrafish were cultured in a breeding tank and mated next morning. Healthy embryos were collected within 1 h after the light was switched on, and then washed with the deionized water. At 4 h post-fertilization (hpf), the embryos were examined under a dissecting light microscope (SZ760) and normally developed embryos were selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exposure and fluorescence record of rGOQDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of rGOQDs were prepared using deionized (DI) water as solvent. Then, 50 embryos (at 4 hpf) were cultured in dishes with 10 mL rGOQDs, and DI water was used as control. Three replicate trials were conducted for each group. The embryos were exposed to different concentrations of rGOQDs (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL) for 7 days and renewed the exposure solutions daily. During rGOQDs exposure, the distribution of rGOQDs was observed under the Nikon SMZ18 NIS-Elements BR Stereo fluorescence microscope. The hatching rate (at 72 hpf), the heart beats (at 96 hpf), and the malformation rate of embryos (at 96 hpf) were recorded. The developmental parameters of larvae at 120 hpf, such as survival rate and body length, were also detected. Tg(cyp1a:gfp) zebrafish were treated the same as AB strain in rGOQDs solution, and all parameters were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total RNAs isolation and quantitative RT-PCR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The total RNAs extraction was carried out from 30 zebrafish larvae at 7 dpf for each group in triplicates by using RNAiso Plus reagent (Takara BioInc., Shiga, Japan) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Then, the concentration and quality of the total RNAs were determined by using a spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) and 1% agarose-formaldehyde gel electrophoresis with ethidium bromide staining. The reverse transcription kit (PrimeScript RT reagent Kit with gDNA Eraser, Takara Bio, Shiga, Japan) was used to synthesize cDNAs with 1 μg total RNAs for each sample according to the protocol recommended by the manufacture. The primers of the target genes in this study were designed by using Primer 3 software (http://primer3.ut.ee/). The primers were shown in the Table 2. The RT-PCR was performed in triplicates on the Applied Biosystems 7300 Detection System (Perkin Elmer Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA) by using the SYBR® Premix Ex Taq™ II PCR Kit (Takara Bio Inc, Tokyo, Japan).

After exposure to rGOQDs solution, the total proteins were extracted from 30 zebrafish larvae using the total protein extraction kit (Aidlab Biotechnologies Co. Ltd.). Western blotting were performed as described [36]. Mouse monoclonal antibody to detect GFP were obtained from TransGen Biotech (ProteinFind™ Anti-GFP, TransGen Biotech Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The secondary HRP antibody was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

All values were expressed as the mean ± SD (standard deviation) of three replicate samples. Statistical analysis was performed by using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). The diagrams were produced using GraphPad Prism (Version 5.0).

As illustrated in the Fig. 1 , the modified Hummer's method was employed to synthesize the GO and the DMF hydrothermal treatment approach was used to reduce the GO and enhance the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared rGOQDs. During this process, we raised the ratio of potassium permanganate and graphite power to 6:1 to enhance the degree of oxidation. Meanwhile, DMF acted as a nitrogen source and reducing agent to enhance the PL properties of rGOQDs. Finally, the QY of the as-prepared rGOQDs was detected as high as 24.62% (Fig. S1 and Table S1), which far surpassed the as-prepared GO (6.53%). At the same time, TEM and AFM analysis of the final products showed that rGOQDs displayed a spherical particle morphology with the 10 nm in lateral size distribution and the 1 nm in height size distribution (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XPS characterization of rGOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the functional groups on the surface of rGOQDs, the FT-IR and XPS were performed. The FT-IR of graphite, GO and rGOQDs were shown in the Fig. 2 A, which recorded the functional groups on the materials. The two small peaks at 2852 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CH2 and CH3, and several peaks around 1450 to 1500 cm−1 were skeletal vibration of graphite aromatic rings for all three materials. Noticeably, the broad peak around 3400 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups for GO, which indicated that the oxidation process endowed the hydroxyl groups on the surface. While there was no strong peaks around 3400 cm−1 for rGOQDs, it demonstrated that the DMF treatment process obviously reduced the hydroxyl groups. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration of CO/COOH at 1652 cm−1 and CO around 1094 cm−1 for GO and rGOQDs were stronger than graphite. Moreover, the peak of CN groups (stretching around 1252 cm−1) of rGOQDs was the strongest among the three materials, which confirmed the nitrogen-doping of rGOQDs.

The XPS was carried out to further confirm the functional groups of rGOQDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the result of XPS showed that a dominant peak for graphite at ca. 284.8 eV, which was attributed to the Cls (98.05%). In addition, two dominant peaks for GO at ca. 284.8 eV and 532.2 eV were ascribed to C1s (61.08%) and O1s (38.92%). However, for rGOQDs, there were three dominant peaks at ca. 284.8 eV, 532.5 eV and 400.1 eV because of the C1s (64.94%), O1s (28.20%) and N1s (6.86%) respectively, indicating the existence of different content of C, O, N in those three materials. Meanwhile, the C1s and O1s profile of graphite, GO and rGOQDs, as well as N1s profile of rGOQDs were deconvoluted into several signals to investigate the components of the materials (Fig. 2CI). As shown in Fig. 2CI and Table 1, the high-resolution spectrums of C1s were deconvoluted into three component of CC/CC (∼284.6 eV), alkoxyl COC (286.6 eV), and carbonylic CO (288.1 eV), which were consistent with the previous reports [37]. Interestingly, the measured spectrum of the GO was divided into two strong peaks of carbon and oxygen, while the oxygen content of rGOQDs was lower than GO. The CN peak was occurred at ca. 285.6 eV, indicating the reduction and N-doping in the rGOQDs. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of the three materials illustrated that the oxygen was almost present in the CO (∼531.4 eV) and CO (∼532.6 eV), and the percentage composition of CO was higher than that of CO. In addition, the N1s profile was deconvoluted into pyridinic N (399.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.3 eV), which confirmed that the nitrogen was successfully doped into the carbon skeleton of graphene. The functional groups of rGOQDs determined by XPS were well consistent with the FT-IR results, which confirmed the functionalization of rGOQDs from graphene through the oxidation and DMF modification processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and PL analysis of rGOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of rGOQDs. As illustrated in the Fig. 3 A, GQDs showed diverse UV–vis absorption peaks. For the precursor GO, two main absorbance peaks occurred at 231 nm and 304 nm. According to the previous reports [38,39], the peak at around 230 nm was attributed to the π–π* transitions of CC, and the shoulder peak at ca. 300 nm was ascribed to the n–π* transitions of CO bond. However, the shoulder disappeared after DMF modification, which indicated that the CO bond was reduced by DMF to a certain extent. As shown in Fig. 3B–D, the emission PL spectrum of the as-synthesized rGOQDs and the precursor GO, as well as the normalized PL spectrum of rGOQDs with progressively longer excitation wavelengths in 20 nm increments from 300 nm to 500 nm were determined. The fluorescence properties of GQDs were attributed to the synergistic effect of electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect, and zig-zag sites. But, the green emission was mostly attributed to surface defects [40]. Theoretically, the green luminance of the as-synthesized rGOQDs (Inset of Fig. 3D) might also be ascribed to the surface defects formed by the DMF hydrothermal treatment. It was noteworthy that the PL intensity of rGOQDs was stronger than that of GO (Fig. 3C and D), which was consistent with the QY result, and the normalized PL spectra of rGOQDs displayed excitation-dependent luminescence and concentrated in the green (around 500 nm) emission part. All above results indicated that the rGOQDs possessed the possibility of fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution and bio-imaging of rGOQDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the as-prepared rGOQDs possessed outstanding optical properties, the distribution and bio-imaging of rGOQDs were implemented by exposing zebrafish embryos in rGOQDs solution. Moreover, the rapid development and transparency of zebrafish embryos facilitate the screening and visual observation of rGOQDs distribution. Multicolor fluorescence imaging of zebrafish after exposure in rGOQDs solutions was shown in the Fig. S3 and Fig. 4 . The capabilities of rGOQDs as fluorescent biomarker were verified by exposure zebrafish embryos at 4 h post-fertilization (hpf) to the different concentrations of rGOQDs solution (0, 25, 50, 100 μg/mL). It concluded that the fluorescent intensity of zebrafish increased with the enhancement of the concentration (Fig. S3). Thus, as a result of the small size of rGOQDs (average in 10 nm), which were smaller than the nanoscale pores on the zebrafish chorion (approximately 0.17 μm2) [41], the rGOQDs could enter the pores of the chorion by simply immersing. It can be observed that the zebrafish exhibited blue, green and red fluorescence under different excitation fields, and the multicolor fluorescent rGOQDs enabled the in vivo observation of their distribution in zebrafish embryos and larvae.

The study of the fluorescence bio-imaging in different time points confirmed the spatial-temporal distribution of rGOQDs. When the soaking period was prolonged to 144 hpf, the multicolor florescent imaging was still observed. However, as the incubation time went on, the rGOQDs had different affinities to the tissues of zebrafish. It displayed that the fluorescence distributed in the whole embryos in the early period, but after 48 hpf, the fluorescence only distributed in the abdomen of the larvae (Fig. 4A–D). This phenomenon indicated that the embryos were more sensitive to the xenobiotics, and the rGOQDs had high biocompatibility and stability during 7 d incubation, which indicated that rGOQDs may be used as candidates for in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developmental toxicity and the perturbation of AhR pathway induced by rGOQDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: The wide range applications of graphene and graphene quantum dots in biomedical and biological fields had attracted much attention of the potential hazards to human and other biological systems [20,42]. Zebrafish is a powerful and effective model to evaluate genotoxicity and developmental toxicity. Therefore, the developmental toxicity of as-prepared rGOQDs in zebrafish was systematically examined by exposing zebrafish embryos at 4 hpf to the different concentrations of rGOQDs for 168 h. As shown in the Fig. 5 and Table 3 , there were almost no significant influence on zebrafish after exposure to the low concentrations (0, 25, 50 μg/mL). However, at high concentration (100 μg/mL), although the rGOQDs exerted no significant influence on hatching rate, body length and mortality, the heart beats were significantly affected. Moreover, the malformation induced by the highest concentration of rGOQDs were shown in Fig. 5B–D. The pericardial edema, vitelline cyst, bent spine were induced by the highest concentration of rGOQDs, and the abnormal morphological features implied that rGOQDs had some potential toxicity hazard to zebrafish.

According to the above results, the highest concentration (100 μg/mL) of rGOQDs induced the development toxicity and teratogenic effect in zebrafish to some extent. Whether can the lower concentrations of rGOQDs affect the inner signaling pathway of zebrafish? The quantitative RT-PCR analysis was performed to detect the genes expression of AhR pathway in zebrafish. As shown in the Fig. 5I–L, the expression levels of cyp1a, cyp1c, cyp7a1 and hsp70 were up-regulation in a dose-dependent manner. Furthermore, in the highest concentration of 100 μg/mL exposure group, the expression levels of cyp1a, cyp1c and cyp7a1 were significantly up-regulated by 8.49, 4.65 and 5.93 fold, while the level of hsp70 was up-regulated 3.63 fold, compared to control (0 μg/mL), indicating that the rGOQDs disturbed the AhR pathway in zebrafish.

To verify the induction of rGOQDs for translational expression of corresponding genes in zebrafish, the Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish were introduced to evaluate the impact. As shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S4, rGOQDs really induced an increase in GFP expression promoted by cyp1a in the treated zebrafish, which were consistent with the result of the real-time PCR. Interestingly, the Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish showed stronger fluorescent intensity with fluorescence expression in the whole body, while wildtype zebrafish (AB line) only showed the fluorescence concentrated in the abdomen (Fig. 6A), which indicated that the rGOQDs may fail to enter the tail and the backbone section. For the Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish, there was an obvious fluorescent expression in the trunk, tail, spine and the head part as well as a significant increase of GFP expression, because rGOQDs perturbed the AhR pathway in zebrafish. The green and blue channels were both implemented to figure out the difference between wild type and Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish after exposed in rGOQDs at 24, 48 and 120 hpf. Moreover, the relative fluorescent intensity were measured quantified by the fluorescent microscope (Fig. 6B), which concluded that the green fluorescence of Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish was significantly higher than the wild type zebrafish.

Moreover, the western blot analysis were carried out to specifically determine the green fluorescent protein expression in the Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish. As shown in the Fig. 6C, the western blot analysis showed that GFP expression was in a dose-dependent manner with the treated concentrations, and was consistent with transcriptional level detected by the real-time PCR and the fluorescence imaging results, confirming the perturbation of AhR pathway by rGOQDs in zebrafish.

In this study, rGOQDs were successfully synthesized using the modified Hummer's method, and DMF was used as a reducing reagent to enhance the PL properties of rGOQDs. As a result, the as-synthesized rGOQDs were highly oxidized by KMnO4 and nitrogen-doped by DMF, which were confirmed by the FT-IR and XPS analysis. The results of FT-IR and XPS showed that the as-synthesized rGOQDs were functionalized with the epoxy/alkoxy groups, carboxyl/carbonyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and CN groups on the surface. The precursor GO was graphene oxide in nature, which contained graphitic sp2 carbon domains with sp3 hybridized carbon atoms bound to oxygen groups, resulting in the enhancement of the solubility and photoluminescence. After DMF hydrothermal treatment, the GO was reduced and nitrogen elements was doped into the layer of graphene oxide with oxygen content of 28.20% and nitrogen content of 6.86%. The previous literature reported that N-doped graphene showed tunable properties in the electrochemical and electrocatalytic fields [43–45]. Similarly, in this study, the PL analysis and QY results confirmed that the fluorescent property of rGOQDs was much better than that of GO. The QY of rGOQDs (24.62%) far surpassed the GO (6.53%) because of the doping of nitrogen, which demonstrated that the DMF treatment endowed the outstanding PL properties of rGOQDs. Moreover, the excitation-dependent luminescence of rGOQDs also enabled them the properties of multicolor bio-imaging.

Due to the outstanding optical properties of the as-prepared rGOQDs, rGOQDs had the great potential for practical application of bio-imaging in living organisms. However, the health hazards of rGOQDs should be systematically investigated before their application. Zebrafish is an ideal model to evaluate the health hazards of the as-synthesized rGOQDs. Our results indicated that the fluorescent intensity was increased with the elevated concentration of rGOQDs (Fig. S3), and zebrafish body absorbed the rGOQDs in a concentration-dependent manner. Moreover, the fluorescence distribution and alteration in zebrafish was traced for 7 days (Fig. 4), and rGOQDs were mostly distributed in the yolk sac of embryos and the abdomen of the larvae. Above bio-imaging results implied that rGOQDs were competent for being used as multicolor bio-probes in zebrafish in vivo without considering its toxicity hazard. Interestingly, Wang et al. found that GQDs were transferred from the solutions into the heart of zebrafish embryos [20]. However, our result indicated that rGOQDs could not specifically transfer into zebrafish heart. The reason may be the difference of GQDs.

To deep explore the health hazards of rGOQDs in zebrafish, the toxicity pathway beginning with the initial perturbation and following through to the eventual biological result should be systematically investigated in this study. RNA-seq whole transcriptome analysis was done to understand the molecular regulatory network caused by rGOQDs exposure (data not shown). The perturbation of toxicity pathways in zebrafish larvae after exposure to rGOQDs pointed to the AhR pathway. Thus, multiple genes related to the AhR pathway were validated by real-time PCR. Moreover, a specific transgenic zebrafish Tg(cyp1a:gfp) was applied to monitor cyp1a level caused by rGOQDs according to the fluorescence intensity in zebrafish. Our results showed that three P450 family genes (cyp1a, cyp1c and cyp7a1) and hsp70 in zebrafish were up-regulated in a dose-dependent manner after exposure to rGOQDs (Fig. 5I–L). As the green fluorescent protein expression represented endogenous cyp1a expression in the Tg(cyp1a: gfp) zebrafish [46], the induced fluorescence indicated that rGOQDs really disturbed the AhR pathway, which was also confirmed by the western blot analysis (Fig. 6C).

In fact, other toxicity pathways were also activated by rGOQDs. How they regulated each other deserves us for further studies. Our on-going studies on transcriptomic response to rGOQDs and the mechanism of molecular regulatory network will be revealed more details. Taken together, rGOQDs with high QY of 24.62% were synthesized, and the perturbation effect of rGOQDs on the AhR pathway in zebrafish were successfully detected in this study. To our knowledge, this is the first time to investigate the genotoxicity of graphene-based materials in mRNA and protein level on the AhR pathway in zebrafish, which will guide us how to avoid the potential hazards of rGOQDs before use for biomedical engineering fields.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the supports from the Three Hundred Leading Talents in Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Chongqing (to D.S.P.), the Key Application and Development Program of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (Grant No. cstc2014yykfC20004 to D.S.P), the Hundred Talents Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (to D.S.P.), the Application and Development Program of Science and Technology Commission of Beibei District, Chongqing (Grant No. 2015-07 to D.S.P), the CAS Western Light Program 2015, and the Basic Scientific Research Program of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (Cstc2016jcyjA0314 to T.S).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2017.04.026.
